sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Measles is caused by *Morbillivirus* (Paramyxoviridae). It is one of the most contagious infectious diseases known (R0 12–18). Unvaccinated or under-vaccinated individuals — including some overseas-born workers — are at significant risk.

**Incubation:** 10–14 days (range 7–21 days) from exposure to rash

**Infectious period:** 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset

**Notifiable disease.** One case on a remote mine site is a public health emergency — early notification and isolation are critical.

**Transmission:** Airborne — virus can remain suspended in the air for up to 2 hours in a room previously occupied by an infected person.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Prodrome (days 1–4) — Highly Infectious**
- High fever (≥38.5°C, may reach 40–41°C)
- **Cough, coryza (runny nose), conjunctivitis** — the 3 Cs
- **Koplik spots** (pathognomonic): tiny white/grey spots on buccal mucosa opposite molars — appear 1–2 days before rash

**Rash (day 3–5)**
- Maculopapular, **begins on face/hairline → spreads downward to trunk → extremities**
- Coalesces on trunk
- Lasts 5–6 days; fades in the same order it appeared
- Concurrent fever often spikes with rash onset

**Complications**
| Complication | Notes |
|---|---|
| Otitis media | Most common (1 in 10) |
| Pneumonia | Leading cause of measles death; 1–6% cases |
| Encephalitis | 1 in 1000 cases; ~15% mortality |
| Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) | Rare, fatal; years after infection |
| Febrile seizures | Especially in children |
| Death | Higher in immunocompromised, malnourished, infants |

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**High-Risk Groups**
- Unvaccinated (including those with only 1 MMR dose)
- Infants <12 months (not yet vaccinated)
- Immunocompromised
- Pregnant women (increased risk of miscarriage, preterm birth, low birthweight)
- Malnourished, Vitamin A deficient

**Mine Site Context**
- One confirmed case triggers mandatory public health notification
- Identify all contacts — anyone in the same building, dining room, or vehicle during infectious period (4 days pre-rash to 4 days post-rash)
- Airborne precautions essential — virus persists in enclosed spaces (vehicles, donga rooms, meal rooms)
- Suspect measles in any febrile patient with rash + cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, especially if overseas-born or unvaccinated

**PPE**
- **P2/N95 respirator** (standard surgical masks are insufficient — measles is airborne)
- Gloves
- Eye protection
- Gown
- Apply surgical mask to patient if tolerated (limits aerosolisation)

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
**Apply P2/N95 before contact**

**Uncomplicated measles:**
- Antipyretics (paracetamol or ibuprofen — avoid aspirin)
- Ensure adequate oral hydration
- Isolate from non-immune contacts immediately
- Notify receiving facility before transport (airborne precautions required on arrival)

**Indications for urgent transport:**
- Dyspnoea, hypoxia (pneumonia)
- Seizures or altered conscious state (encephalitis)
- Signs of sepsis/secondary bacterial infection
- Pregnancy
- Infants <12 months
- Immunocompromised
- Signs of dehydration with inability to maintain oral intake

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
**Measles Pneumonia**
- Administer oxygen; titrate to SpO2 ≥94%
- IV access for IV fluid support if required
- Minimise AGPs in vehicle (nebulisers increase aerosolisation)
- Antiviral/antibiotic therapy is hospital-initiated — do not delay transport

**Measles Encephalitis**
- Airway management if GCS depressed
- Manage seizures per Seizure CPG
- IV access; observe for raised ICP signs (Cushing's triad)
- Urgent P1 transport

**Vehicle Management**
- Keep vehicle ventilation set to directed exhaust
- Vehicle must not be re-used until full decontamination is performed (virus can survive in air for up to 2 hours)
- Document exposure times and notify infection control

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- **Measles is a notifiable disease** — notify WA DoH within 24 hours; this triggers contact tracing
- Two MMR doses provide ~97% protection; employers should consider vaccination verification during pre-employment health checks
- Post-exposure prophylaxis options (for non-immune contacts within 72 hours of exposure): MMR vaccine; or Normal Human Immunoglobulin (NHIG) for high-risk groups within 6 days
- **Vitamin A deficiency** increases severity — consider supplementation in high-risk unvaccinated travellers (hospital-initiated)
- Do not return confirmed or suspected cases to shared accommodation — isolate until 4 days after rash onset
